It is a national political and economic plan to establish a transitional provisional government after the fall of the ayatollahs. The Iranian activist guarantees that “this detailed roadmap for national recovery” includes a plan for “the first 100 days after the collapse of the regime and the long-term reconstruction and stabilization” of the country and “has the support of numerous business leaders from around the world”. “A democratic Iran would transform the Middle East, converting one of the most persistent sources of unrest in the world into a pillar of regional stability”, says Pahlavi, who praises the “historic” Abraham agreements, promoted by Trump in his first term.

item.title: Crime | Study reveals perception of increase, even with downward trend
item.description: Although official data reveals fewer crimes in the Macao SAR, this reality is not reflected in society's perception. A study reveals that in Macau only 13 percent of respondents were aware of the drop in crime and that more than 35 percent believed that crime had increased The study “Examining fear of crime among Macao residents: Vulnerability, victimization and situational factors”, published in the journal International Review of Victimology, reveals that in the territory the population does not fear crime, although perceptions regarding crime are not always in line with official data. The work, authored by Yixuan Wang, Donna Soi Wan Leong and Lichao Lu, from the University of Macau (UM) and Jianhong Liu, from the Macau University of Science and Technology (MUST), reveals that there was, between 2021 and 2022, a “13.9 percent decline in crime”, according to data released by the Security Services Directorate in 2023. However, “the survey data from this study reveals a significant discrepancy in perception: only 12.83 percent of respondents reported being aware of the decline, while 35.72 percent believed that crime had increased,” it reads. Furthermore, “the vast majority of respondents did not demonstrate emotional fear” in the face of crime, as “78.87 percent reported zero unsafe places and only 2.77 percent identified two or more” unsafe areas in Macau. Meanwhile, “only 9.69 percent of respondents reported feeling at risk of victimization, indicating that the vast majority did not feel at risk”. The study also shows that “the proportion of residents who reported defensive behavioral responses was the lowest among the fear dimensions, with only 2.89 percent of respondents indicating that they had adopted defensive behaviors in the previous 12 months”. The authors describe how Macau constitutes a paradigmatic example for analyzing social fear of crime and perceptions regarding official data, as it is a special administrative region that “functions under the ‘one country, two systems’ governance framework, characterized by a distinct sociopolitical and legal structure, along with a multicultural population”. Furthermore, it is mentioned that Macau has an economy “that depends exclusively on gambling and tourism, which drives rapid financial circulation and large-scale population mobility”, being a “small urban territory with an exceptionally high population density”. In this way, the particular characteristics of the territory “raise questions about the applicability of existing models of fear of crime, since residents' perceptions of crime and disorder can be shaped by dense urban environments and rapid population flows”. A question of age The study was carried out based on the 2022 Macau Victimization Survey, managed by the Macau Society of Criminology, a project funded by the Macau Foundation. The target population of the survey were residents over 18 years of age, and for this study, additional telephone interviews were carried out with 1,102 people, resulting in a final sample of 795 people. This study also states that “the results reveal consistencies and deviations in relation to previous research”, as “contrary to what has been established, age and gender do not significantly influence the fear of crime”. For the authors, the “unexpected result” can be explained by “institutional and Macau-specific factors”, as the “Macau Government has placed great emphasis on investment in social well-being, which can act as a buffer against fear for potentially vulnerable groups”. The measure of financial support for the repair of buildings is highlighted, such as the Building Maintenance Support Scheme and the Subsidy Scheme for Maintenance of Common Parts, which “further reduces the need for residents to implement additional defensive strategies at the domestic level”. The study highlights the “extensive welfare infrastructure in Macau”, such as “universal healthcare, old-age pensions and social protection schemes, which can mitigate perceived vulnerability among the elderly”. Laws that help Regarding gender-based violence, it is described how the Domestic Violence Law and “strengthened support systems for victims can contribute to a feeling of security associated with gender”. In 2022, there were 22 cases of rape and eight of sexual coercion, according to data from the Institute of Social Action (IAS) cited in the study. The authors also highlight that Macau's Gender Inequality Index was 0.06 in 2021, “substantially lower” than that of mainland China (0.192), Japan (0.083) and the global average (0.457). This indicates “a relatively equitable environment in terms of gender”, according to mentions made by the IAS in 2021 and the United Nations Development Program, in 2022. The academics describe how “these factors, taken together, may mitigate the relevance of physical vulnerability in the formation of fear of crime, explaining the lack of significant effects of age and gender”. The study highlights that the fact that “consistently low levels of fear” regarding crime can be “partially attributed to the Macau Government's substantial investment in public security and the high density of police resources”. It appears, therefore, that “the strong policing and investment in public security in Macau”, even with the low crime rate, results “in an equally limited prevalence of victimization in the sample”. Furthermore, "the relatively low levels of fear reported by Macau residents can be explained by both substantive cultural mechanisms and methodological factors. At the substantive level, collectivist and relational cultural orientations can exert a genuinely buffering effect", since in Chinese societies "individuals are embedded in dense family, kinship and community networks", which can facilitate "access to social support and mitigate stress and fear responses", the study reads. Signals to the Executive The academics argue that these results “present several implications for social governance in Macau”, given the existence of a “paradox between residents’ subjective perceptions of crime and the decline reflected in official statistics”. All of this “underlines the importance of reinforcing transparency in the dissemination of information and law enforcement”, with “crime-related information being disseminated through multiple channels and improving the visibility of prevention efforts”, which can “contribute to reducing fear and strengthening public trust”. The Government's actions in the area of ​​security seem to produce some results, the study describes, which is “particularly relevant in the context of rapid population aging”. “In addition to maintaining these investments, strengthening informal social control can also contribute to increasing the collective effectiveness of communities. Initiatives such as the expansion of volunteering programs and support for neighborhood associations (e.g. Federation of Residents' Associations of Macau; Association of Youth Volunteers of Macau) can provide additional social resources to mitigate risks associated with physical vulnerability”, it is argued.

item.title: China/Guinea-Bissau | A relationship that began with decolonization
item.description: In “Relations between the People's Republic of China and Guinea-Bissau: between diplomacy, economy and security”, academic Orazio Maria Gnerre describes a path that began with Guinea-Bissau's independence. The article, published in the academic journal Janus, speaks of mutual interests that allow “the near future of Guinea-Bissau and China” to remain “intimately linked” At a time when China is seeking to cement relations with the so-called “Global South”, namely Africa and Latin America, commercial and diplomatic links with Portuguese-speaking African countries have been increasing. Guinea-Bissau, a country that has been experiencing internal political tensions, has been one of China's African partners. The relationship was analyzed by academic Orazio Maria Gnerre in the article “Relations between the People's Republic of China and Guinea-Bissau: between diplomacy, economy and security”, published in the magazine Janus – E-Journal of International Relations, from the Universidade Autónoma de Lisboa. The author describes how the relationship between the two countries began in 1975, when Guinea-Bissau gained independence from Portugal, after 13 years of Colonial War, and how these connections to the People's Republic of China (PRC) are far from over, taking into account the existence of mutual economic interests, especially because “the PRC outlined an important investment plan in Guinea-Bissau”. The author concludes that “relations between China and Guinea-Bissau”, cemented “in a long period of contemporary history, from the context of the Cold War to the present day, in a phase of increasing multipolarization”, occurred “in a double way”. In other words, there has been “a diplomatic dialogue permitted by a certain type of political-cultural attitude and by a realistic approach in relation to the economic and commercial needs of the two countries”. In this relationship between states, “the colonial issue is not of secondary importance”, taking into account that the decolonization process provided “a framework of political significance for cooperation between the two countries (which, therefore, also occurs in areas beyond the merely economic)”, in addition to having guaranteed “the development of a certain type of international trade”. In the Portuguese decolonization process, 51 years ago, “China entered the political game of Portuguese-speaking nations and those that were under Portuguese rule, including Guinea-Bissau”, having started to develop during this period “the idea of ​​South-South trade”, launched at the Bandung Conference in 1955. It is important to emphasize that, in these years after decolonization, Guinea-Bissau broke relations with the PRC to strengthen ties with Taiwan, something that ended in 1998. In these years in which China had a relationship with the African country, “economic exchanges between the two countries” took place, with the PRC “financing the construction of a stadium, a hospital and other facilities”. More recently, Guinea-Bissau joined the “Belt and Road” initiative, having signed, in 2021, a memorandum of understanding with China. "This type of project could certainly provide Guinea-Bissau with some advantages, if it were fully used to increase investments for development. Furthermore, guaranteeing the construction of infrastructure projects, such as ports, highways and railways, is of great importance for Sub-Saharan African countries", he analyzed. One of the most recent agreements focuses on the mining sector, with “an important agreement” having been signed with Chinalco. “The partnership (with the Belt and Road Initiative project) manifests itself not only in the construction of infrastructure, but in commercial alliances”. Not everything is roses Orazio Maria Gnerre considers that “the near future of Guinea-Bissau and China appears to be closely linked”, even though there have been “ups and downs” in this relationship. The volatility focuses on “the difficult period” experienced by Guinea-Bissau “in terms of growth and development”. "Political tensions and the impact of natural disasters make it difficult to build solid international relations that allow the country to develop better infrastructure and assert itself both in the region and globally. The complex relations between the People's Republic of China and the country also fit into this context." Therefore, “this element must be taken into account, considering the important presence of the Asian country on the African continent, as well as the business volume and support for the construction of infrastructures that it guarantees to many African nations”. It is described that, in this context, “diplomatic relations between the African country and its political parties with China have been fluctuating and marked by internal issues, as well as international events”. Today the African continent “is growing, both economically and in terms of infrastructure” and Guinea-Bissau also fits into this growth, being a country that, after 1975, “was initially led by the authority of the national liberation movement and, subsequently, entered a phase of serious political confrontations and relative instability”. It is, however, a nation with “a lot of resources and a great potential for growth that must only be used in the best possible way”, which is why the Guinean authorities seek for the country to grow “in the field of infrastructure and build its own economic-political autonomy based on the sectors in which it finds the greatest advantages”. Thus, “it is in this context that we can insert and interpret the relations between the sub-Saharan country and the People's Republic of China”. In its history, Guinea-Bissau “received help from the Asian country [China] in this delicate process [of decolonization], which favored alternating diplomatic relations between these two political entities”. According to the author, “the theoretical profile of Chinese politics has always addressed both the group of countries emerging from colonization, and the idea of ​​cooperation for development between decolonized countries”, which is why “the relations between Guinea-Bissau and China are so important and so strongly characterized in a political sense”. Internationalization is the key The author and academic highlights “two trends that come together almost spontaneously” in the relationship between China and Africa. “On the one hand, China seeks to establish partnerships with African countries, both to follow the premises of its international economic policy — formulated since the beginnings of the revolutionary communist government — and to acquire, through trade and cooperation, raw materials of great importance for the country's industry.” Then there is “the attempt by many African countries to grow and guarantee more and more stability and security”, with “internationalization, access to international markets and the sale of their raw materials becoming a necessity for these countries”. Internationalization, through trade and export of products, is “of vital importance, as is attracting foreign investment”. Thus, the academic states that the political history of Guinea-Bissau determined all these political and economic trends that, to date, are relevant within the scope of a complex analysis of the country's current situation, as well as its specific relationships. “The real weight that the phenomenon of decolonization had conditioned, in all aspects, the form that the country, its trade and its diplomatic action took”, creating a scenario of comparison “with contemporary China, also born from a phenomenon of decolonization”, despite the necessary historical and contextual differences. This panorama ended up creating, between the two countries, “a certain type of economic-political relationship that intervenes in a context that, without a doubt, is not easy (both at a regional and global level), but which is advancing at an accelerated pace in a global situation undergoing complete restructuring”. The article also cites data that show how Guinea-Bissau has registered some development, despite going through “alternate phases of success in terms of economic growth”, thanks to great volatility “due to events of a political or environmental nature”. Furthermore, the author recalls that Guinea-Bissau “is frequently hit by climate shocks”, and in 2003, 2004 and 2005 it suffered “serious floods that damaged its infrastructure, villages and the agricultural sector, leaving a serious impact on the economy and society”.

item.title: UN | Human rights under large-scale attack
item.description: Human rights “are under attack on a large scale throughout the world”, carried out “by those who hold the greatest power” and, sometimes, even “with pride”, denounced the Secretary-General of the United Nations yesterday. "The rule of law is being replaced by the rule of force. And this attack does not come from the shadows nor is it a surprise. It is happening in plain sight and, often, led by those who hold the greatest power. All over the world, human rights are being deliberately and strategically repressed, and sometimes with pride", declared Guterres, speaking in Geneva at the opening session of the 61st session of the UN Human Rights Council. The secretary-general warned that the consequences of this attack “are devastating”, pointing out that people are suffering “twice: first with violence, oppression or exclusion, and then again with the world’s indifference”. "We live in a world where mass suffering is ignored, where human beings are used as bargaining chips, where international law is treated as a mere inconvenience. Conflicts multiply and impunity has become contagious. This is not due to a lack of knowledge, tools or institutions. It is the result of political choices", deplored the former Portuguese Prime Minister. According to António Guterres, “this crisis of respect for human rights is not an isolated case” and “reflects and magnifies all other global fractures”, as “humanitarian needs are exploding, while financing is collapsing”, “inequalities are increasing at an impressive speed, countries are drowning in debt and despair, and climate chaos is accelerating”. The United Nations leader also highlighted that “technology, especially artificial intelligence, is increasingly being used in ways that suppress rights, deepen inequality and expose marginalized people to new forms of discrimination, both online and offline”. "On all fronts, those who are already vulnerable are being pushed further to the margins. And human rights defenders are among the first to be silenced when they try to warn us", he lamented, reinforcing that, "in this coordinated offensive, human rights are the first victims". Deplorable portrait Exposing a devastating portrait at a global level, the UN Secretary-General pointed out that today an “increasing tightening of civic space” is evident, with “journalists and activists imprisoned, NGOs [Non-Governmental Organizations] closed, women's rights in retreat, children's rights ignored, people with disabilities excluded and democracies in erosion”. "The right to peaceful assembly is crushed - and I once again condemn the recent violent crackdown on protests in Iran. Migrants are persecuted, detained and expelled, with complete disregard for their human rights and humanity. Refugees are made into scapegoats. LGBTIQ+ communities are vilified. Minorities and indigenous peoples are targeted. Religious communities are attacked," he continued. Alluding to his personal history, the last time he spoke as secretary-general at the opening of a session of the Human Rights Council, he pointed out that “growing up under the Salazar dictatorship” taught him that “the denial of human rights corrodes all aspects of society. “When the Security Council is paralyzed, when vetoes serve as a political shield, when geopolitical rivalries prevail over the protection of civilians, the result is the same: impunity spreads, suffering multiplies and human rights are trampled on,” he said. “We need a Security Council that reflects the world of today, not the world of 1945,” he added.

item.title: Rota das Letras | Film by Filipa Queiroz tells the story of homeless people from Alta de Coimbra
item.description: “Salatinas – Stories from the Old Alta of Coimbra” brings journalist Filipa Queiroz back to Macau, together with Rafael Vieira and Tiago Cerveira, co-authors of the documentary that is part of the Rota das Letras poster. The film tells the story of how, during the Estado Novo, around three thousand residents were expelled from Alta de Coimbra to allow the construction of the University City The University City in Alta de Coimbra is one of the city's tourist attractions, offering a stunning landscape. However, despite the beauty, the history of the construction of Cidade Universitária is full of suffering. In the documentary “Salatinas – Histórias da Velha Alta de Coimbra”, Filipa Queiroz, Rafael Vieira and Tiago Cerveira tell the story of the people who lived there in the 1940s and who found themselves suddenly homeless and without a plan B. The film is part of the poster for the Rota das Letras Literary Festival, which starts on March 5th. “We hope that, in Macau, the story can have an impact on everyone and in particular on the many people from Coimbra who we know reside in the territory”, Filipa Queiroz, who lived in Macau for several years, told HM and TDM – Macau Television. He is happy to return and to reveal this story unknown to many. And he gives the greatest praise to a festival that saw its birth 15 years ago. "I find the programming of this edition of Rota das Letras particularly courageous and relevant taking into account the contexts of Portugal, Macau and the world at the moment. We try to present the film where it makes sense and this is precisely the case. It is a film about people, but also about power, resistance, identity and the price of progress – themes that Macau knows so well", he highlighted. Filipa Queiroz came into contact with the story of these people through a report. “Rafael Vieira collaborated with me as a journalist, as he ran a publication in Coimbra [Coimbra Coolectiva] and told me that there was an [interesting] story there, and that he wanted to tell stories outside the box, more invisible, so to speak.” “Salatinas” was born, in reference to the name given to the people who lived in Alta de Coimbra, where there were already faculties of Literature, Law and the library. However, the Estado Novo evicted these people to build the currently existing buildings. Three thousand people left their homes, making this “one of the biggest urban transformations in Portugal, at a time when forced evictions, lack of access to housing and social segregation still make headlines”, describes the documentary’s synopsis. “Salatinas” is, therefore, “a trip down memory lane of the historic neighborhood wiped off the map under the regime’s impetus for renewal and the hubbub of students, tricanas, grocers and tailors that hides beneath the colleges”, adds the same synopsis. Leave quickly “What happened was the will of the Government at that time”, says Filipa Queiroz, to explain how a political decision changed the lives of so many people. "The University of Coimbra moved between Lisbon and Coimbra for many years, but then it was located in Coimbra and was the only one in the country, where the country's brains, the elite, were. There was then a decision that the university needed to grow and it had to be there. Salazar himself had studied there [Faculty of Law], in addition to there being a hospital. It wasn't about requalification, but they really destroyed what there was to build on top." What followed was a profound transformation in families in the area. Many never fully recovered. "When the destruction began, basically the Government warned the people who lived there that they all had to get out of there. They gave values ​​to the houses [for compensation], completely disproportionate and much lower than the value of the houses." "There is a person we mention in the film who was part of Salazar's circle, she was a doctor and an illustrious person, and she managed to have her house be one of the last to be removed, but she was still [homeless]. The poorer and more humble the people were, the quicker they had to leave, with one hand in front, the other behind. These people were thrown into neighborhoods of the city, which we can't even call neighborhoods, because it was something improvised at the time", says the co-author of the documentary. We are talking about areas with “fragile” houses. However, the precariousness of housing already came from behind. "Little houses were built that weren't of incredible quality, but the houses in Alta de Coimbra weren't there either, many of them. There was no basic sanitation, that needed to be requalified." A “self-identity” Filipa Queiroz highlights how “the solution was to end everything, not valuing the fact that it is a neighborhood, and with its own identity, and this is the main issue”. The neighborhood was Salatina. "Salatinas are people who worked or lived in the upper part of the city, or who were born there, and who crossed paths daily with the student elite. This diversity was very rich, which ended up having effects on the cultural aspect, on music, for example." Coimbra is a land associated with names in Portuguese music, especially interventionists, such as Zeca Afonso and Carlos Paredes, and among the “doctors” there were also “the futricas”, that is, “the people of Coimbra, but no one ever remembered those people”. Filipa Queiroz highlights that “many people, including those from Coimbra, don’t know what happened there”. “There was a city there, a very concentrated urban network full of people and life, with São João bonfires, cultural events, all very identity-related things.” All things destroyed with the requalification and expansion of the University City. Filipa Queiroz highlights that nowadays, and despite university experiences, the city's Alta no longer has the dynamics it once had. “It has that austere architecture and at night, nothing happens.” “Salatinas” gave voice to a topic that is rarely talked about. "People are very moved and feel very valued. We had people who went to the premiere [in Coimbra, in the Alta area] and who told us: 'It seems that there has been a repair here'. This even led to the death of some people, because they committed suicide out of grief, because they had to leave the place where they built their business and their lives". Filipa Queiroz also thanks Ricardo Pinto, director of Rota das Letras, for the invitation made. "He invited us knowing only the idea of ​​the film. This will allow my colleagues to discover, for the first time, this place, Macau, which is so special to me." Filipa Queiroz wants to continue telling stories through images. Having made documentaries linked to Macau, such as “Boat People” and “Once Upon a Time in Ka-Hó”, the idea is to continue in an audiovisual genre so close to journalism. "Documentaryism adds an artistic layer and time that journalism does not allow. Yes, I would like to make [more documentaries], that is always on my horizon. There are always challenges, with financing the work and the time that can be dedicated to it, but without a doubt, yes, I would like to continue."

item.title: Heritage | Stories of the stores on Rua Cinco de Outubro told in a book
item.description: Natalie Fu has just released a book about traditional commerce on Rua Cinco de Outubro, at the invitation of the Macau Cultural Heritage Reinvention Studies Association. “Macao’s Historical House Taxonomy – The Shophouses at the Rua Cinco de Outubro” tells the centuries-old story of shophouses that were the “heart of Macau’s Chinese community” A book has just been published that reveals a little about the history of commerce carried out by the Chinese community on Rua Cinco de Outubro, located in Porto Interior, the scene of many commercial movements over the centuries. The work also explains how these stores, which are at the same time houses, reveal details about the history of Macau. “Macao’s Historical House Taxonomy – The Shophouses at the Rua Cinco de Outubro”, written by Natalie Fu, a doctoral student at the University of São José, was invited by the Association for Studies on the Reinvention of Cultural Heritage of Macau, whose edition also depends on “the support and assistance of many other people”, the author told HM. One of the book's sales outlets, which does not have a Portuguese version, is Pin-to Livros. “The investigation began with the love for Macau and its old buildings”, reveals the author, who began the research work around these buildings in 2022, in the middle of the pandemic. "Rua de Cinco de Outubro has always been an important commercial area in Macau. Once, it was the heart of Macau's Chinese community. Even today, many old stores remain close to the local neighborhood. The stores are mixed-use buildings", explains the author. This is the stage for commercial diversity, as Rua Cinco de Outubro “is home to several businesses, such as traditional Chinese bakeries, Chinese medicine stores, tea shops and fruit shops”. According to the presentation of the work, made on social media by the association itself, this type of property constitutes “a common type of multifunctional residential building”, where the “plan of the buildings is generally rectangular, with two to three floors”. Typically, the ground floor is used for the store or as a warehouse, while the upper floors are reserved for housing. "This type of construction can adapt to the local climate, presenting great flexibility in the construction methods and materials used. Due to the diversity of daily uses, shop houses were once the main type of building on the streets of Macau, having important historical, social, cultural, architectural and aesthetic value", it further reads. Preservation Challenges Still according to the same note, the book draws attention to the importance of preserving this type of construction and, consequently, a symbol of local heritage. “Unfortunately, because they are small-scale and scattered buildings, and because they are not fully protected by the Macau Heritage Protection Law, they have been gradually disappearing with the rapid development of society”, and, currently, “only a small number of these buildings have the classification of ‘monument’ and are protected”. For Natalie Fu, “the preservation of Macau's stores faces many challenges, one of which is the fact that they are not included in the existing list of protected old buildings in Macau”, in addition to that, in her view, “changes in construction laws have also made it difficult to maintain these old buildings”. Asked about the future of this iconic street in Porto Interior, the author recognizes that “the growth of a city inevitably involves the renovation and disappearance of buildings”, so “determining which buildings should be preserved and how to preserve them is a long-term challenge”. Natalie Fu even talks about the need to extend the investigation to more streets in Macau that are also traditional shopping areas. "Research and documentation of the stores on Rua Cinco de Outubro and in Macau are urgent as their number is dwindling. Additionally, additional academic investigations and discussions with the local community are needed to inform the future direction of the Inner Harbor area." In relation to the panorama on Rua Cinco de Outubro, the author describes that maintenance should be focused on, keeping commercial spaces open. "Stores that are kept in use better highlight their social value. Taking some stores on Rua Cinco de Outubro as an example, the existence of century-old stores further emphasizes the cultural and historical value of the area and the stores. Furthermore, 'keeping them in use' allows for more immediate monitoring and maintenance, which is the most direct way of protecting ancient architecture." A certain influence In a territory characterized by the coexistence of Portuguese and Chinese cultures, Natalie Fu's book shows how these houses were influenced by other cultures. “Chinese and Western architecture in Macau have always influenced each other throughout historical development, and this feature is particularly visible in the appearance of the shop houses.” In this way, this book helps to cement this characteristic, “establishing a classification system for Macau’s shop houses, consolidating their architectural definition”. This book is, therefore, a “classifying study of the old shop houses in Macau”, constituting “the first step towards their safeguarding”, describes, in the same note, the association that supported the editorial and research project. "The author carried out a detailed record and survey [of the stores], starting with visits and cartography surveys in the Porto Interior area. Maps were drawn up of the location of the shop houses on that street, analysis sheets based on fieldwork, photographic records and interviews with current traders and residents, in order to understand the history of the use of each building." Furthermore, the work documents seven cases in particular, “providing information on their history, evolution and current use, as well as a preliminary discussion on possible preservation directions”. The book also records two examples of shop houses that have now disappeared. “It is hoped that this work will encourage more people to discover the ‘beauty of Macau.’ With the shop houses as a central theme, it is expected to promote exchange and discussion between academics from different areas”, the association also points out. Natalie Fu invited “several experts and academics to share their ideas, experiences and suggestions on the protection and future of this type of buildings”, it is described. Born and raised in Macau, Natalie Fu had the opportunity to visit all of Southeast Asia from a young age, trips that led her to “foster a deep appreciation and passion for architecture”. The author has a degree in Architecture, also describing herself as a person interested in the area of ​​heritage conservation and a “historical architecture scholar trained in Macau”.

item.title: Natural Disasters | Chan La Kei calls for more extreme exercises
item.description: Representative Chan La Kei wants to know whether security forces will carry out more extreme simulations to respond to natural disasters and bad weather. The matter is addressed through a written interpellation, published on the Legislative Assembly portal linked to the Fujian community. “Will the authorities create more extreme simulated scenarios, taking into account the weaknesses detected last year, such as ‘consecutive occurrence of tropical storms in Macau, causing pressure on respective resources’, ‘obstacles in cross-border material support’, etc., in order to assess the interdepartmental and interregional dynamic response and prevention capacity?”, asked the legislator. In the same vein, Chan La Kei questions the authorities about plans to create localized alerts for different areas of the territory, depending on the level of impact of natural occurrences, as well as the possibility of using artificial intelligence to produce cell phone messages in several languages ​​to alert those affected. The deputy highlights that the use of several languages ​​in communication is important, because responding to an emergency situation often requires not wasting time. Among the issues raised about emergency response, the deputy also wants to know how authorities are currently able to identify places where unforeseen situations occur and how they can speed up response mechanisms to promptly send security agents to the location.

item.title: Courts | Portugal opens two judge vacancies for Macau
item.description: After the president of the Superior Council of the Judiciary (CSM) expressed openness to sending more judges to Macau in October last year, the Portuguese authorities have now opened a recruitment competition The Superior Council of the Judiciary (CSM) of Portugal announced on Tuesday the opening of recruitment of two Portuguese judges for positions in Macau's first instance courts in the civil area. According to the published notice, judicial magistrates with a seniority of more than seven years and less than 15 years can apply, with the appointment being valid for two years, possibly renewable. Applications must be submitted by March 31, and according to the document prepared by the Independent Commission Responsible for the Appointment of Judges of the Macao SAR, the selected magistrates will have a salary and benefits equivalent to those of local judges of the same category and seniority. The salary corresponds to a percentage of the Chief Executive's monthly salary, currently set at 268,297 patacas, which varies according to the length of service as a judge, between 50 percent and 60 percent of the monthly amount earned by the Government leader. In addition to remuneration, judges will be entitled to holiday and Christmas allowances, housing or rental and equipment allowances, medical assistance for themselves and their household, as well as judicial holidays of 22 working days per year. Portugal and Macau maintain a judicial cooperation agreement that ensures the continuity of Portuguese magistrates (judges and prosecutors) in the territory, supporting the Portuguese-based legal system. This collaboration focuses on recruitment, training and the exchange of judicial experiences. Bridging exits The only judge from Portugal currently working in Macau is Jerónimo Alberto Gonçalves Santos, judge of the Court of Second Instance, after judge Rui Ribeiro brought forward the end of the special commission, which ended in May 2026, to the end of October 2025. In 2024, the CSM rejected the stay of Portuguese judge of the Court of First Instance Carlos Carvalho, who had been in Macau for 16 years and had been invited by the Independent Commission for the Appointment of Judges in the territory to continue for another two years. Despite the rejection, in October last year, during a visit to Macau, the leader of the Supreme Court of Justice, João Cura Mariano, who inherently also presides over the CSM, told journalists that if the Macau authorities showed interest there would be an opening to send more Portuguese magistrates to the territory. “We are going to try to see what is really useful that they can still convey and whether they are necessary, because if they are necessary, new judges will come”, he guaranteed.

item.title: Technology | Sam Hoi Fai wants to promote industrialization
item.description: The Chief Executive considered that technological development in Macau must have practical results and lead to increased productivity and the application of technologies in industry. The statements are contained in the speech given during the first meeting of the Science and Technology Council this year. “The ultimate objective of research and development, as well as investments in the scientific and technological field, is not mere research, nor is investment just to create a superficially attractive ‘technological’ image; the ultimate objective must be the transformation of knowledge into productivity and the industrialization of technologies”, warned Sam Hou Fai. “We must maintain this orientation in order to maximize the practical role and integrated benefits of scientific and technological development,” he added. The Chief Executive also highlighted the need to present results: “on this occasion, I would also like to highlight a specific point: to boost scientific and technological work and research in Macau, it is necessary to establish and put into practice a clear vision of performance”, he said. During the time he spoke, Sam Hou Fai insisted that technological development must follow the Central Government's new five-year plan and be linked to the country's development.

item.title: Tobacco | Government finds it difficult to ban smoking from those walking on sidewalks
item.description: At a time when public consultation is taking place on increasing restrictions on tobacco consumption, the Government is ruling out the possibility of a total ban on smoking and increasing taxes on tobacco The head of the Health Services (SS) Smoking and Alcoholism Prevention and Control Office, Lam Chong, considers it difficult to implement a smoking ban for those walking on the streets. The limitation was recognized during the morning program Forum Macau on the Chinese channel Radio Macau. “We studied practices worldwide regarding people smoking while walking and no country or region has managed to implement this type of ban,” said Lam Chong, after being confronted by a listener who called the program to defend the ban. “The most applicable practices, which are also foreseen in the proposed changes, include classifying areas with the highest flow of visitors as places where smoking is prohibited”, he indicated. Lam Chong acknowledged that the measures do not satisfy the desire of part of the population to prohibit those who walk from smoking. However, there is hope among the Government to help reduce the impact of smoking in outdoor places with greater concentrations of people. At the same time, Lam Chong highlighted that the classification of certain areas as places where smoking is prohibited can become a message to smokers that their addiction is seen as an undesirable act. Anti-tobacco fight In yesterday's Forum Macau program, changes to the tobacco prevention and control regime were discussed, as a public consultation is taking place until 8 April. According to the measures proposed by the Executive, a total ban on any contact with electronic cigarettes, as well as other tobacco products, such as pee, will be implemented. Previously, the Executive explained this option by considering that these types of tobacco products are gateways to the world of drugs. However, the Executive will continue to ban traditional cigarettes, even though yesterday some listeners advocated a ban on all tobacco products or a huge increase in tobacco tax. However, Lam Chong defended traditional cigarettes: “A total ban is not appropriate, because people will smoke in secret”, he stressed. Currently, tobacco tax takes up 60 percent of the retail price. Lam Chong argued that the price should not be increased further through taxes, because the value is lower in neighboring regions, which will lead to more and more smuggling. No magazines Regarding the ban on the possession of electronic cigarettes in public places, Lam Chong pointed out that it will target all smokers, even those who only have this type of equipment stored in a bag. However, the head of the Office for the Prevention and Control of Smoking and Alcoholism indicated that inspectors will not be competent to carry out searches, therefore, situations in which cigarettes are hidden should not lead to fines. Another listener expressed concern that the video recording by inspectors may violate personal privacy. In response, SS deputy director Cheang Seng Ip assured that inspectors only turn on video cameras if there is a conflict and threats during the law enforcement process. The person in charge also explained that before turning on the recording equipment, inspectors will have to inform the individuals involved. And if there were no abnormal occurrences, the recording will be deleted after seven days.

item.title: Chinese MNE says Macau should remain “open to foreign talent”
item.description: The commissioner of the Chinese Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Macau stressed yesterday that, as an international center for cooperation, the city must remain “open to foreign talent”. During an information session on the country's 15th Five-Year Plan and its impact on Macau's development, Bian Lixin highlighted that “visa facilitation and cross-border mobility measures will be strengthened to attract highly qualified professionals”. These include preferential actions that facilitate the cross-border movement of foreign citizens between Macau and Hengqin and visa facilitation policies for foreign citizens with permanent residence in Macau who travel to the mainland, supporting the construction of the territory as an “international center for high-level talent”. The Chinese central government established the Hengqin Deep Cooperation Zone as a special economic area designed to support the diversification of Macau's economy and complement various essential services for the city's residents. Macau established, in July 2023, a program to attract qualified professionals from the financial sector and the areas of scientific and technological research and development, including Nobel Prize holders. The program provides, among other advantages, tax benefits. At the end of October, the secretary-general of the Talent Development Commission said that Macau approved 464 of the more than a thousand applications for the program. The overwhelming majority of staff are from mainland China (80 percent) and Hong Kong (ten percent), with 47 percent having “experience of working or studying abroad”. However, residence in Macau for Portuguese citizens has become more restricted since August 2023, and they are now treated like other foreigners, focusing on family reunification or very specific technical skills. Permanent residence authorization generally requires seven years of habitual residence. Aligned efforts According to the commissioner of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Macau, her office will align diplomatic efforts to ensure that the local development strategy is articulated with national priorities. At the same time, Bian highlighted that Macau must “strengthen its diplomacy”, taking advantage of ties with Portuguese and Spanish-speaking countries to deepen cooperation within the scope of the “One Belt, One Road” initiative and expand exchanges with Eurasia and Southeast Asia. The Macau Government confirmed on Tuesday that the Chief Executive, Sam Hou Fai, will visit Portugal and Spain between the 17th and 23rd of April. Sam Hou Fai, who took office in December 2024, highlighted the promotion of financial and commercial services between China and Hispanic countries as a priority, as a complement to the role of a Sino-Lusophone platform assigned by the Chinese Government to Macau. The 15th Five-Year Plan (2026-2030) for China's development was approved this month by Beijing and focuses on “new quality productive forces”, high technology, technological autonomy and strengthening domestic consumption. The plan sets the objective of moderate economic growth, with Gross Domestic Product (GDP) targets between 4.5 and 5 percent. The Chief Executive confirmed that Macau's development plans will be aligned with the national plan, promising “bold reforms”.

item.title: Study | Renovations transformed the largest gaming center in the world
item.description: A study by academics João Ilhão Moreira and Zhou Yudi indicates that reforms carried out between 2022 and 2024 by the Macau Government aligned the gaming industry with Beijing's broader objectives in terms of capital control The dismantling of Macau's VIP gambling promoter system, once responsible for more than 70 percent of casino revenue, marked a decisive shift in China's governance priorities, according to a study by the University of Macau. The authors, João Ilhão Moreira and Zhou Yudi, concluded that reforms introduced between 2022 and 2024 by the Macau Government aligned the gaming industry with Beijing's broader goals of greater capital controls, enhanced transparency and law-based governance. The study states that the end of these VIP promoters (known as “junkets”) reflected a strategic alignment with “mainland China objectives, including greater capital control”, strengthening financial transparency and law-based governance. The work highlights that the “junkets” model, which served high-level clients and facilitated opaque capital flows from mainland China, has become increasingly incompatible with the application of anti-money laundering rules and ongoing modernization. “Although it has been integral to Macau’s growth, the junket system has come into increasing conflict with the enforcement of anti-money laundering rules and the modernization of the sector,” the authors wrote. The study traces the evolution of the industry since the transfer of sovereignty in 1999, noting that junkets were tolerated for decades due to their contribution to the game's gross revenue. Reality of numbers In 2011, gaming revenue in Macau reached 269.1 billion patacas, five times the revenue of Las Vegas, and with VIP rooms contributing more than 70 percent of that revenue. The arrest and conviction, in 2021, of Alvin Chau, leader of the largest group of “junkets” in the territory, the Suncity Group, symbolized the end of an era and accelerated the transition to a gaming market model centered on occasional players and integrated tourism. Chau was later sentenced to 18 years in prison, having been found guilty of more than 100 crimes, including leadership of a criminal association, fraud and illegal gambling exploitation, involving bets exceeding HK$823.7 billion. According to researchers, the “junket” system evolved into a powerful financial network, which extended far beyond the gaming rooms. The operators were not only intermediaries for VIP clients, but also facilitators of large opaque capital flows between mainland China and Macau. The authors pointed out that VIP lounges and junkets “created networks of unregulated capital flows”, establishing parallel channels outside China's official banking system. These networks have become problematic as Beijing has tightened capital controls and demanded greater transparency. The study highlights that, although tolerated for years, junkets depended on side bets and discreet transfers of funds, practices that ended up colliding with China's modernization objectives. In 2022, the Macao SAR Government advanced the first major review of the Gaming Law in more than two decades. The reform introduced profound changes: it redefined the role of concessionaires and gambling promoters, practically dismantled the “junkets” system and reinforced control and transparency mechanisms. Path to diversification The new legal framework began to favor the diversification of the industry, with a greater focus on the mass market and integrated tourism, to the detriment of VIP lounges, dominated by “junkets”. Furthermore, it aligned Macau with China's national priorities, namely strengthening capital control, preventing money laundering and integrating the MSAR into the national economic development strategy. “Legal and regulatory measures introduced between 2022 and 2024 were designed to align Macau’s gaming industry with China’s priorities,” the study concludes.

item.title: Health | IA bus route opens between Macau and Mainland China
item.description: The first artificial intelligence (AI) autonomous bus route dedicated to cross-border medical travel was opened between Macau and Mainland China, local media reported yesterday. According to China Daily, the bus service connects the Hengqin border post and a hospital operated by Guangzhou Medical University in the same area. Powered by autonomous driving technology and with operational support from national companies MOGOX and Autonoma, the route is seven kilometers long and allows passengers to reach the hospital in around 15 minutes. This type of AI buses can operate without human intervention in specific conditions and circuits and is increasingly integrated into China's official public transport networks. During the trial period, the service is open to the public free of charge, operating from 8am to 12pm and from 1:30pm to 5:30pm, with departures every 30 minutes. Official data shows that more than 20,000 visits were made last year by Macau residents seeking treatment at this hospital, which has 21 clinical departments. MOGOX said it has already deployed its autonomous buses in more than 20 cities in China, serving more than 200,000 passenger trips and accumulating a total of five million kilometers traveled. In October 2025, the company also won the competition for the first official autonomous bus project in Singapore, marking the first time that this type of vehicle was incorporated into a public transport system outside of China.

item.title: Between Hegemony and Vulnerability
item.description: “Power is not revealed by striking hard or often, but by striking true.” Honoré de Balzac The military presence of the United States in the vast geopolitical space that stretches from the Mediterranean to the Indian Ocean constitutes one of the central pillars of contemporary international security architecture. This region, marked by a succession of strategic seas with the Mediterranean, Black Sea, Caspian, Red Sea, Persian Gulf and Indian Ocean, concentrates essential energy resources, vital maritime routes and friction zones where the interests of regional and global powers intersect. The arrangement of American bases in this geographic arc is not only the result of immediate operational needs, but reflects a long-term vision of the role of the United States as a global power and guarantor of stability in areas considered critical for the functioning of the world economy. The current configuration of this military network is inseparable from the historical evolution of American foreign policy since the end of the Cold War. The collapse of the Soviet Union opened a period of unprecedented hegemony, often described as unipolar, during which Washington sought to consolidate its force projection capabilities and secure privileged access to sensitive regions. The 1990s marked the beginning of a phase of frequent interventions, justified for humanitarian reasons, collective security or the defense of strategic interests. The Gulf War, operations in the Balkans and interventions in the Horn of Africa illustrate this trend. However, it was after 2001, with the launch of the so-called “war on terrorism”, that the American military presence reached an unprecedented scale, requiring an extensive logistical and operational network that extended throughout the region. The strategic geography of American military bases follows a logic of control of maritime corridors, proximity to conflict zones and rapid response capacity. In the eastern Mediterranean, cooperation with countries such as Greece, Turkey, Egypt and Jordan makes it possible to maintain air and naval capabilities that guarantee permanent surveillance over the Levant, Black Sea and Suez Canal. This area functions as a gateway to the Middle East and as a connecting platform between Europe, North Africa and Western Asia, being particularly relevant due to its proximity to areas of instability such as Syria and Lebanon. In the Persian Gulf, the core of the American military presence, the concentration of oil reserves, the vulnerability of energy routes and rivalry with Iran justify the maintenance of air, naval and command bases in countries such as Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates. These infrastructures enable surveillance, deterrence and intervention operations, in addition to functioning as logistical support points for missions in Iraq, Syria and Afghanistan. In the Indian Ocean and the Red Sea, the American presence ensures control of maritime routes that connect Europe to Asia, being crucial for monitoring threats such as piracy, terrorism and political instability. The Djibouti base, although located outside the traditional Middle East core, plays a central role in the regional security architecture. The expansion and maintenance of this network of bases must be understood in light of a broader phenomenon, which is the increasing militarization of American foreign policy. This process has been interpreted as a symptom of structural fragility. As the United States' ability to shape the international order through diplomatic, economic and institutional instruments has declined, dependence on military means to preserve influence and credibility has increased. The multiplication of interventions since the beginning of the 21st century highlights this trend. The prolonged campaigns in Afghanistan and Iraq revealed the limits of American military superiority, both in terms of strategic effectiveness and human and financial costs. The difficulty in achieving lasting political objectives, despite massive investment in military resources, contributed to an erosion of the perception of invincibility that had characterized the 1990s. Continued military involvement had profound repercussions on US domestic politics. The exponential increase in military spending, largely financed by debt, coincided with a period of political polarization and loss of confidence in institutions. The discrepancy between the declared objectives of the interventions and the results obtained fueled an intense debate about the role of the United States in the world and the sustainability of its global strategy. The war in Afghanistan, prolonged for two decades, has become a symbol of the difficulties faced by Washington when trying to transform complex political realities through military means. The intervention in Iraq, in turn, exposed weaknesses in risk assessment and post-conflict planning. The combination of high costs, uncertain results and political weariness contributed to an environment of strategic introspection. American deterrence, which for decades has been based on perceived technological superiority and global responsiveness, faces significant challenges. The emergence of new powers, the proliferation of access denial technologies and the growing assertiveness of regional actors have reduced the United States' room for maneuver. The military presence, although still extensive, does not automatically guarantee the ability to influence adversary behavior. The transformation of the strategic environment is visible in the way countries in the region have tested the limits of American tolerance, resorting to indirect attacks, hybrid warfare and low-intensity operations. The multiplication of incidents involving American bases and forces demonstrates that the military presence, in itself, does not ensure the deterrent effect it once had. Despite the difficulties, the network of bases continues to perform essential functions for United States foreign policy. Among its main uses are ensuring freedom of navigation in vital maritime corridors, monitoring transnational threats, supporting regional allies and the ability to quickly respond to humanitarian crises or emerging conflicts. The military presence thus functions as a risk management instrument in an environment characterized by volatility and competition between powers. However, analysis of the layout of the bases reveals a structural tension between the declared objectives of American foreign policy and the results obtained. The intention to promote stability and security often contrasts with the persistence of conflicts, regional rivalries and humanitarian crises. Military presence, although necessary to protect strategic interests, can also generate perceptions of interference and fuel anti-Western narratives, limiting the effectiveness of diplomatic and economic approaches. The future of the American military presence in the wider Middle East will depend on the evolution of relations with regional powers, the ability to adapt to new war technologies, the internal situation of the United States and the dynamics of the international system. The tendency towards a redistribution of strategic priorities, with greater attention to the IndoPacific, may lead to adjustments in the network of bases, but will hardly result in a complete abandonment of the region. The energy importance, the centrality of maritime routes and the persistence of conflicts make a substantial withdrawal unlikely. However, it is possible that Washington will seek to reduce direct exposure, favoring regional partnerships, joint operations and less visible forms of presence. The way in which these factors come together will determine the role of the United States in the region in the coming decades and will influence the stability of one of the most sensitive spaces in the international system.

item.title: Portraits of famous men I knew
item.description: I once saw Couto Viana, reduced to coutos, in a (neo-salt)az(ar)ist homage to the palace of independence I once saw Cesariny at Lux, at the Assírio & Alvim party, with his finger in the air I once saw herberto helder having a beer in galegas and i didn't have the courage to join him. approach, like Moses from the burning bush I once saw Manuel da Silva Ramos running away from a demonstration, saying he was a surrealist I once saw my father at the bend in the road, losing his mind I once saw António Barahona kissing the forehead of the deceased Gil de Oak, as the women say. book religions Once I waved to Alexandre Vargas across the street, in front of the Indian woman's house, and he didn't returned my greeting I once drank vermouth with Eduíno de Jesus, Pedro Ivo and Maria João Ruivo once I saw my grandfather, who was a homosexual carpenter in Angra do Heroísmo, cooking me a knife I once heard Eduardo Lourenço in Aix-en-Provence complaining about his sister-in-law's snoring Once I pretended I didn't know Manuel Cintra I once heard joaquim manuel magalhães tell an anecdote about his wife days ago eugénio de andrade The first time I saw António Cândido Franco he was wearing shorts, like a schoolboy Portuguese youth the first time I saw Cruzeiro Seixas was amazed when I told him that the nephew of Easter was beautiful Fernando Grade once put me in his “dog book” because I didn’t have the money to pay him magazine that was foisting on me Once I saw my mother enveloped in a mass of shadows, twisted and trembling

item.title: Margarida Vila-Nova's play
item.description: By Gonçalo Alvim The monologue “At first sight”, starring Margarida Vila Nova and directed by Tiago Guedes, was on display this Saturday, March 14, at the Macau Cultural Center. I had the opportunity to watch it and, like most of the audience present, I liked the performance and the plot, adapted from the work of Suzie Miller. At the end of the play there was a conversation with the actress and the director, and the audience was challenged to ask their questions. And so we were able to understand different opinions, questions that the topic raises, and what the protagonists think about the message transmitted. For those who haven't seen it, the play deals with the theme of sexual abuse of women, telling the fictional story of a criminal lawyer, Teresa, who, having successfully defended suspected sexual aggressors, ends up being sexually assaulted by a colleague herself, in a relationship that began by chance and consent and ends in non-consensual rape. Teresa, despite realizing the legal fragility of her case, courageously decides to take it to court, but she is unsuccessful in her attempts and her colleague leaves unharmed. The play does not intend to bring immediate solutions, it simply shows, from two different angles, the drama experienced by victims of sexual abuse. In effect, a contrast is made between the security of those who know how to play with the current legal system – Teresa as a criminal lawyer –, with the weaknesses of those who see themselves in the role of the victim – Teresa after having been sexually abused. Although the general feeling in the room was one of approval, and there was even a suggestion to adapt the play to schools with the aim of raising awareness among younger people about the problem of non-consensual relationships, it seems to me that the play involves, on the one hand, some dangers for society – especially for younger people; and, on the other hand, it deserves important reflections that have not been considered. As the protagonists said, the purpose of the play is largely to raise questions and provoke reflection, not to provide solutions. And that's what I'm going to try to do. Among the risks I see in the piece is the exposure of the limits of the legal system, where cases like this, especially when well guided by the defendant's defense, normally lead to acquittal. Highlighting this aspect is Teresa's actions in the acquittal of her clients, cynically exposing the weaknesses of justice and the natural inconsistencies of victims who went through situations of rape; and then there is his own case, where, despite his experience and knowledge of the legislation, he is unable to present irrefutable evidence, falls into inconsistencies and ends up failing to secure Francisco's conviction. And so, after more than two years of suffering with the case, he finds himself a victim of the system he used and leaves the Court emotionally destroyed. Some of those present in the audience suggested that the path should involve changing some of the jurisdiction, but the truth is that this is very difficult to put into practice and involves risks – given the current system of presumption of innocence until proven otherwise. And so, this disclosure of the imperfections of the legal system, with no solution in sight, runs the risk of bringing, on the one hand, a distrust in justice on the part of victims, who conclude that it is not worth pursuing the accusations; and, on the other, a feeling of impunity on the part of potential violators, now realizing that if they do things in a certain way, without evident evidence, they will not suffer consequences. The burden of proof lies with the victim and they do not even have to testify in court. Another worrying aspect for me, as a father, is the insecurity transmitted to girls in relation to boys, as well as the boys' feeling of impotence in counteracting this distrust placed upon them. Ultimately, this could lead to the creation of walls between each other, not the relationships of trust that society needs to grow. The protagonist of the play even mentions that one in three men commits, or has committed, sexual abuse, which made me look around and estimate that, if that were the case, there would be several dozen men in that room in that room. The piece does not intend to provide solutions, as Tiago Guedes highlighted in the conversation, but only to lift the veil on a problem that persists in society. In addition to being well interpreted, the story is well created and well put together, highlighting two aspects that were not mentioned by the public but that should be at the center of a conversation with young people on the topic. The first has to do with building relationships. Teresa barely knew Francisco, she only knew that he was from a more privileged social background than hers and had concluded, from the legal cases he had defended, that he would have a promising future. However, perhaps with some ambition in the mix, she let things rush too quickly, thus entering that limbo of not really knowing what kind of relationship they had, but without this leading her to back down, letting the relationship run aimlessly. This is part of a tendency to seek immediate gratification, to the detriment of a more solid relationship, which requires time and space for discernment. In the play, Teresa's mother watched in court the exposition of this dizzying development of their relationship, and apparently remained undaunted. We realize that she must have suffered, because this is not what a mother wants for her daughter. The second important aspect in understanding the story, and which is directly related to the first, was the excessive consumption of alcohol by Teresa and Francisco on different occasions, which contributed to the precipitation of the events that unfolded. As we know, alcohol disinhibits, lowers our defenses and, in excess, can even change us beyond recognition. Francisco, who is initially presented as a little shy, got involved with Teresa at the office after they consumed too much alcohol; later, during the non-consensual act, they would both be quite drunk. It is not important here to discuss whether this fact mitigates the seriousness of what happened, but to alert, especially younger people, to the dangers involved when drinking alcohol is abused. Drinking too much leads to situations that exceed the drinker's ability to control, and this fact must be taken into account not only when we drink, but also when others who are with us have drunk. The situation may be beyond our control and we should be alert. In conclusion, I really enjoyed the play but I was relieved that my children didn't go. If that had happened, I know I would have had long conversations with them to frame what they had seen and devalue what was being insinuated. And when at the end the protagonist, in a very direct way, suggested that we look left and right, on the mathematical assumption that someone next to us had been involved in a non-consensual sexual act, I assure you that I looked carefully to see if anyone was doing it. Nobody did. I concluded that, in general, those who watched the play trust each other. Not everything is lost.

item.title: From wife to porn actress: Opening of traditional marriage (II)
item.description: Last week, we talked about the story of a married woman in her thirties, reported by the Japanese media, who almost died after undergoing surgery for a fracture. This experience made her reflect on what she had not yet achieved in life. Due to the prolonged absence of marital intimacy, he felt emotionally and physically needy. After several discussions, the couple agreed to continue living in the same house and maintain a traditional relationship, sharing domestic tasks. However, something has changed; the husband agreed that his wife would start acting in adult films, to show off her beautiful and voluptuous body, a fact that he would appreciate and even evaluate her performance. With this change, the woman's physical needs and emotional emptiness would be filled by other people. Thus, this relationship went from “traditional” to “open” marriage. An “open marriage” results from a mutual agreement, for which detailed rules are established. For example: Is openness limited to meeting other people or does it include sexual activity? Can the “other” be a friend of the couple? Can you be invited home? Are contraceptive and preventive measures necessary? Psychological incompatibility is one of the reasons for choosing an open marriage. In this case, one of the partners has a greater sexual desire than the other and open marriage can serve as a compensatory mechanism to maintain marital stability. Furthermore, more than preventing the repression of instincts or infidelity, conditional openness can be used to build a relationship of trust because “there is nothing to hide”. In an open marriage, if one member of the couple needs the other, the third member must leave immediately. This way it is guaranteed that the status of the spouse is superior to that of the lover. Although open marriage has advantages and appears more “free,” it actually faces significant challenges. First, few people remain unfazed when confronted with their spouse's intimacy with other people. Jealousy can change initial consent as opposed to open marriage. Second, from a legal perspective, open marriage can be understood as a violation of the obligation of marital fidelity and a challenge to social norms. This story is a typical “existential” case. The post-surgery “near-death experience” drastically altered this woman’s values, causing her to no longer conform to society’s expectations. For her, participating in adult films was more than just a sexual activity; It was a kind of “special snapshot of memories,” which reflected his intense desire to preserve his youth and prove his existence. However, turning to the highly commercial adult film industry is an extremely stereotypical form of self-renewal. An open marriage does not imply participating in adult films, only she will know why she made this decision. Many people get into this industry to make a living, but this woman did it to create “memories”. When one of these films becomes available online, it is no longer possible to delete the images, which could be a weight she will carry when she wants to assume a different social identity. Furthermore, how will the couple's friends and family view her after seeing these films? What kind of pressure will the husband suffer? Are they both prepared to accept the different pressures and consequences that this decision will bring to them? Maintaining multiple relationships requires great time management skills. While one member of the couple is having affairs with other people, will the other be able to take care of the children and fully fulfill family responsibilities? Should this couple think about having more children? This duo completely separated “family functions” from “sexual needs,” challenging the limits of morality. While we must respect the choices that adults make with mutual consent, this is undoubtedly a high-risk decision. Is this merely an isolated case in Japanese society, or does it represent a new trend in the understanding of marriage in Japan? When traditional marriage can no longer withstand an individual's self-renewal, should we repair the system or reshape its boundaries? This will be an inevitable problem for Japanese society and law in the future. Finally, if this woman can trust others to engage in sexual activities with them, who is she truly in love with? Did you find the love of your life in marriage? Legal Consultant of the Association for the Promotion of Jazz in Macau Associate Professor of the Faculty of Management Sciences of the Macau Polytechnic University cbchan@mpu.edu.mo

item.title: FRC | Poetry and Reading Featured Today
item.description: The Rui Cunha Foundation (FRC) presents today, starting at 6:30 pm, the session “An Encounter of Poetry: The Tree and everything they want”, which aims to celebrate World Poetry Day, a date established by UNESCO at the 30th General Conference in Paris, in 1999. The event also marks the International Day of Forests, with both dates to be marked on March 21st by the United Nations. Today's session is organized by the Reading Club of the Associação dos Amigos do Livro in Macau, and, as described by the FRC in a statement, “an opportunity to encourage reading, support poets and preserve linguistic and cultural diversity, through the written and spoken word”. The session, to be led by doctor and writer Shee Vá, aims to encourage the public to share poems that have a special meaning in their lives, which may or may not be subject to the “Tree” theme. What matters is “honoring poetic expression and its cultural importance as an identity legacy of the language of each country or countries”. "Poetry, with its ability to move, provoke reflection and transmit feelings, has been able to cross centuries and civilizations, being one of the pillars of literary art. If you want to participate, show up at the FRC Gallery and ask to speak. Come prepared to listen and read, contributing to enriching the experience of local communities in this aim of giving voice to the feelings of poets and touching souls", describes the same note about the event.

item.title: Cinema | Four Portuguese co-productions highlighted at the Hong Kong festival
item.description: Four Portuguese co-productions will be part of the lineup at the Hong Kong International Film Festival, which will take place between the 1st and 12th of April. “O Riso e a Faca” by Pedro Pinho, “Magalhães” by Lav Diaz, “Amílcar” by Miguel Eek and “Last night I conquered the city of Thebes” by Gabriel Azorín join Manoel de Oliveira’s classic “Aniki-Bóbó” According to the schedule of the Hong Kong International Film Festival (HKIFF), released on Tuesday, the World Cinema category includes the film “The Laughter and the Knife”, the second fiction feature film by Portuguese director Pedro Pinho. The co-production between Portugal, Brazil, France and Romania was a candidate for the Goya 2026 – Spanish film awards – for best European film and for the 2026 European film awards from the European Film Academy. “The Laughter and the Knife”, by Pedro Pinho, with Sérgio Coragem, Cléo Diára and Jonathan Guilherme in the cast, premiered in May at the Cannes Film Festival, in France, earning the actress a representation award in the “Un Certain Regard” section. In the Masters and Authors section, “Magalhães”, by Filipino director Lav Diaz, will be shown, about the Portuguese navigator Fernão de Magalhães, who was the Philippines' candidate for an Oscar nomination for Best International Film. The film, starring Mexican actor Gael García Bernal as the Portuguese navigator Ferdinand Magellan (1480-1521), who embarked on a circumnavigation journey and died in the Philippines. “Magalhães”, with Portuguese co-production by Rosa Filmes, also features actress Ângela Machado, in the role of Beatriz Barbosa, the navigator's wife, and actors Tomás Alves, Rafael Morais, Ivo Arroja and Valdemar Santos. In the company of masters In the same section, there is the most recent documentary by German Werner Herzog, “Ghost Elephants”, recorded in Angola, and which premiered at the 82nd edition of the Venice International Film Festival. Among the documentaries to be shown at HKIFF is “Amílcar”, which is the result of a five-year work around the independent African politician Amílcar Cabral, made by Spanish director Miguel Eek. The co-production between Spain, France, Switzerland, Cape Verde and Portugal, by Lx Films, has photography direction by João Pedro Plácido, and is nominated for the award for best Ibero-American documentary at the Guadalajara Film Festival, which will take place in April. In the Kaleidoscope – Poesia em Movimento section of the HUFF will be “Last night I conquered the city of Thebes”, by Gabriel Azorín, a Spanish production with the Portuguese First Age. The festival will also present several classic films restored in 4K, including “Aniki-Bóbó” (1942), the first film by Portuguese director Manoel de Oliveira (1908-2015). The HKIFF includes a competition for short films, the Firebirds awards, and one of the nominees is “Samba Infinito”, a co-production between Brazil and France, directed by Brazilian Leonardo Martinelli.

item.title: Architecture | CURB once again organizes photography competition
item.description: CURB – Center for Architecture and Urbanism is once again organizing the Macau Architectural Photography Competition, with the theme “People in Architecture”. Interested parties can submit proposals until May 3rd, with two categories available: the open competition and the competition for students CURB – Center for Architecture and Urbanism has once again launched a challenge to photography fans as part of the fifth Macau Architectural Photography Competition. Interested parties can submit works that reflect the way architecture comes to life with human presence until May 3rd. “People in Architecture” is the theme of the competition that invites photography enthusiasts to explore the relationships between people and the spaces they inhabit and the ways in which everyday movements, activities and interactions shape the urban environment. "Architecture is more than form or structure, it gains meaning through the people who interact with it. Designed to welcome life, meetings and moments of reflection, spaces come to life through everyday interactions.
